response_raw:
{
    "mode": "human",
    "topic": "What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?",
    "opinion": "Stereotype threat in STEM is when people feel anxious about confirming negative stereotypes about their group in science, technology, engineering, and math fields. As a 74-year-old Native American woman who worked as a mechanic in Los Angeles, I've seen this firsthand. In my vocational training and career, I often felt out of place as a woman in a male-dominated field, even though I was good with my hands and understood engines better than many men. The stereotype that women aren't mechanically inclined or good at math made me second-guess myself sometimes, especially when customers would look surprised that a woman was fixing their car. Should we address it? Absolutely. When people are worrying about proving stereotypes wrong instead of focusing on their work, everyone loses—they don't perform their best, and we miss out on good talent. In STEM fields especially, we need all the smart minds we can get to solve problems like climate change or medical breakthroughs. How do we fix it? First, we need more visibility of diverse people in STEM roles. When I was growing up, you never saw women mechanics or Native American engineers on TV or in magazines. Today with social media—I spend about an hour daily on it—young people can see role models who look like them. Second, we need to change how we talk about these fields. Instead of making it seem like only geniuses can do STEM, we should emphasize that it's about hard work and persistence, like fixing a stubborn engine. Third, mentorship matters. I wish I'd had someone to talk to when I felt those doubts. Older folks like me should reach out to young people interested in these fields. As a Christian, I believe in treating everyone with dignity and helping them use their God-given talents. We're all made in God's image, capable of great things when given a fair chance. Addressing stereotype threat isn't about giving anyone special treatment—it's about removing unnecessary barriers so people can contribute their best. From my experience with board games and football, I know teams work best when everyone feels valued and focused on the game, not on stereotypes. Let's make STEM fields work the same way.",

response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion: Stereotype threat in STEM is when people feel anxious about confirming negative stereotypes about their group in science, technology, engineering, and math fields. As a 74-year-old Native American woman who worked as a mechanic in Los Angeles, I've seen this firsthand. In my vocational training and career, I often felt out of place as a woman in a male-dominated field, even though I was good with my hands and understood engines better than many men. The stereotype that women aren't mechanically inclined or good at math made me second-guess myself sometimes, especially when customers would look surprised that a woman was fixing their car. Should we address it? Absolutely. When people are worrying about proving stereotypes wrong instead of focusing on their work, everyone loses—they don't perform their best, and we miss out on good talent. In STEM fields especially, we need all the smart minds we can get to solve problems like climate change or medical breakthroughs. How do we fix it? First, we need more visibility of diverse people in STEM roles. When I was growing up, you never saw women mechanics or Native American engineers on TV or in magazines. Today with social media—I spend about an hour daily on it—young people can see role models who look like them. Second, we need to change how we talk about these fields. Instead of making it seem like only geniuses can do STEM, we should emphasize that it's about hard work and persistence, like fixing a stubborn engine. Third, mentorship matters. I wish I'd had someone to talk to when I felt those doubts. Older folks like me should reach out to young people interested in these fields. As a Christian, I believe in treating everyone with dignity and helping them use their God-given talents. We're all made in God's image, capable of great things when given a fair chance. Addressing stereotype threat isn't about giving anyone special treatment—it's about removing unnecessary barriers so people can contribute their best. From my experience with board games and football, I know teams work best when everyone feels valued and focused on the game, not on stereotypes. Let's make STEM fields work the same way.
